item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, I never said it didn't work. You'll just have to ponder why there might be standing orders from the Administrator to keep thirteen specific individuals alive for an extremely long time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This does a great job of "torture being described in detached language" but I have one question.

What is the in-universe justification for this program?
Even tyrants and murderers have some justification or logic to their acts so what is the justification here?

Or is it in that O5/EC Joint Directive?

item.title: Re: Author Note
item.content:encoded:
I would like to clarify that my description isn't intended to portray this as a bureaucratic blunder. The piece was absolutely written with the intent to be a gross ethical violation masked in flat language.

That said, Sakarya's got a whole lot more suffering in her future. That's what you get for taking upper management positions!

item.title: Re: Author Note
item.content:encoded:
The author may have intended this to come across as bureaucratic tragedy, but, to this audience, it reads as straight-up authoritarian torture, combined with self-serving intellectually-vacuous justifications that get the people in charge what they want without having to do the heavy lifting of defending it to those they claim to serve and, explicitly, while avoiding the justifiably violent wrath of the latter and claiming a monopoly on violence.

Thus, I strongly disagree with the author’s take on their own material. This isn’t a mere bureaucratic snafu, but a straight-up fascist horrorscape.

I approve. +1.

The author is in good company.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a well written example of the horrific being translated into detached, clinical language, letting the emotional impact come purely from the reader's sensibilities.

As I'm always searching for connections and symbolism as I read, the drug name "Pralilapril" in an article about life-extension called to my mind the name of the Ringworld character Halrloprillalar. Referential, or just wild imagination? It wouldn't be the first Niven reference on the wiki.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A portion of its leadership was destroyed, and Sakarya was apparently under the impression that the individuals she had collaborated with to end the conflict could be trusted (or were at least thoroughly intimidated.) One might expect that Alpha-1 serves the office of the Council, not individual members of it, and that they would carry on under new leadership. It turns out they do, just in an unpleasant way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is some really cool stuff. My only nitpick would be that didn't Alpha-1 get destroyed in the skip where this woman and her Ethics committee took control? How would they still be around to do all this, and if it was a remnant of the previous force why were they given unfettered access to a former enemy?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you very much. All I can say at this point is that I appreciate all the kind words when the subject’s so grim.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love your work! You manage to give everything an old-SCP feel while managing to tell a new-SCP-level story, and this is no exception.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's probably the nicest thing anyone's said about my prose in general, so thank you. Bureaucracy-heavy articles get fewer eyes on them in my experience, but I'm glad to entertain anyone at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That was my only nitpick with this. I really enjoyed this skip!

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the style of this prose. It's bold, unapologetic, staunchly intelligent, and daring. It probably and unfortunately will make compromises with its reception because of that, but I personally love it all the more for it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I’ll change the numbers when those series open (or someone else can if I’m not around,) but thank you for the heads up. I’m not a fan of redaction in this narrative context.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I would suggest turning that SCP 6000 number into a redacted skip instead.

I’ve been here quite awhile, and I remember a story that used an SCP 4000 (this was several years ago and this was already an older story, series three was still brand new at this point. It was an interesting story. Something to do with mismeasurements resulting in a containment breach. I believe the author was comforted knowing that the site was unlikely to reach such a high number anytime soon) and look where we are now! Skips are being written in greater numbers as this site grows and more incredible writers join us. You by no means must, however; it may cause confusion when we do eventually make our way up to 6000 skips.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, I don't quite GET it, but I have a theory…

Spoiler inbound
– hide block

It's all an elaborate scheme to gain information from these sunworms after they bond with their host?

Even if I'm wrong, +1.

item.title: Author Note
item.content:encoded:
Trying again from a slightly different angle: Bureaucracy, more bureaucracy, some obfuscating language, bad things, weird relationships and weirder duties.

Also seeing how little description I can get away with.

As always, thanks Petrograd for the feedback.
